items.title: fix(memory): default batch embeddings to off
items.body:
#### Summary Change the default for `remote.batch.enabled` from `true` to `false` for Voyage, OpenAI, and Gemini memory embedding providers. The Voyage Batch API (and equivalents) is designed for large-scale offline corpus vectorization with a 12-hour completion window and 33% cost savings. However, when used as the default for memory reindexing, typical workloads (e.g. ~30 memory files, ~900 chunks) spend 15+ minutes cycling through `validating` → `in_progress` → `finalizing` states before any embeddings are written to the SQLite index. The standard synchronous `/v1/embeddings` endpoint already batches multiple texts per request (up to `EMBEDDING_BATCH_MAX_TOKENS` per call), so disabling the async Batch API does not reduce throughput — it removes the async job submission/polling overhead. The same ~900 chunks complete in under 60 seconds via the synchronous path. Users who need the Batch API for very large corpora (100K+ documents) can still opt in via `remote.batch.enabled: true`. lobster-biscuit #### Behavior Changes - `remote.batch.enabled` now defaults to `false` (was `true`) - Memory reindexing uses synchronous embedding API calls by default - No change when `remote.batch.enabled: true` is explicitly set in config #### Codebase and GitHub Search - `src/agents/memory-search.ts:146` — the default resolution line - `src/memory/manager.ts:1352-1358` — `resolveBatchConfig()` consumes the setting - `src/memory/batch-voyage.ts` — Voyage Batch API implementation (unchanged) - Searched issues/PRs for "batch default" and "voyage slow" — no prior discussion found #### Tests - `src/agents/memory-search.test.ts` — 11/11 pass (3 updated to expect `enabled: false`) - `src/memory/batch-voyage.test.ts` — 2/2 pass (unchanged, tests explicit batch usage) - `pnpm build` — passes - `pnpm lint` — 1 pre-existing error in `src/channels/plugins/onboarding/whatsapp.ts` (unused import, unrelated) #### Manual Testing ### Prerequisites - Voyage AI provider configured (`provider: "voyage"`, `model: "voyage-4-large"`) - Memory files in workspace `memory/` directory ### Steps 1. Delete `~/.openclaw/memory/.sqlite` to force full reindex 2. Restart gateway, trigger a session to initiate memory sync 3. Observe synchronous embedding calls complete in <60s for ~900 chunks 4. Verify SQLite populated: `files`, `chunks`, `meta` tables have rows 5. Verify `memory_search` tool returns relevant results #### Evidence Before (batch enabled by default): - Two Voyage batch jobs submitted for 57 chunks - Stuck in `validating` → `in_progress` → `finalizing` for 15+ minutes - SQLite index remained empty (0 files, 0 chunks) during entire wait - `memory_search` returned zero results After (batch disabled, synchronous): - 925 chunks embedded via ~50-100 synchronous API calls - SQLite fully populated (37 files, 925 chunks) in <60 seconds - `memory_search` returned correct results immediately **Sign-Off** - Models used: Claude Opus 4.6 (via Pi agent) - Submitter effort: 80% agent / 20% human - Agent notes: Root cause identified by tracing logs showing Voyage batch jobs stuck in async processing states. The batch API 12-hour SLA is documented by Voyage AI as intentional for cost optimization, making it unsuitable as a default for interactive memory reindexing.

Greptile Overview

Greptile Summary

This PR changes the resolved default for `agents.defaults.memorySearch.remote.batch.enabled` from `true` to `false` in `src/agents/memory-search.ts`, and updates the associated unit tests to expect batch mode to be off unless explicitly enabled. The resolved `remote.batch` settings are consumed by the memory indexing manager (`src/memory/manager.ts`) to decide whether to use async batch embedding APIs vs synchronous embedding calls during reindexing.

Confidence Score: 4/5

- Mostly safe to merge, but docs/schema defaults are inconsistent with the new runtime default. - The code change is a simple default flip with corresponding test updates, and the batch behavior is still opt-in. The main remaining issue is that user-facing documentation and schema descriptions still state the old default (true), which will mislead users and downstream tooling that relies on schema help text. - docs/concepts/memory.md, docs/zh-CN/concepts/memory.md, src/config/schema.ts

items.title: fix: Finish credential redaction that was merged unfinished
items.body:
#### Summary Replaces hardcoded regex-based credential redaction with a schema-driven approach using `z.registry()`. Each sensitive field is now annotated with `.register(sensitive)` in the Zod schema, making `mapSensitivePaths()` the single source of truth for which fields to redact. Also exports `redactConfigObject()` and applies it in `config.set`, `config.patch`, and `config.apply` responses to prevent credential leakage in write endpoints. Fixes regressions from #9858 where numerical token fields were corrupted, and dashboard config display was broken. #### Repro Steps 1. Configure a channel with a numeric field that has "token" in its name 2. Call `config.get` → observe corrupted/redacted numeric value 3. Open dashboard → observe config UI unable to save #### Root Cause Previous redaction logic in #9858 relied on regex-based `isSensitivePath()` matching, which didn't account for: - Non-string sensitive fields (corrupted numerics) - Env-var placeholders (redacted when they shouldn't be) - Inconsistent redaction between read and write endpoints #### Behavior Changes - Sensitive fields are now identified via `.register(sensitive)` in Zod schemas, not regex patterns - Env-var placeholders (`${VAR_NAME}`) are exempt from redaction (Prep for future changes) - Write endpoints (`config.set`, `config.patch`, `config.apply`) now redact credentials in responses - Keys added by plugins/extensions are still redacted by name, only skipping non-string values now. Changing this would be a breaking change. However, if they supply uiHints with sensitive:false (or true), that is honored. - When the config on disk is broken, we cannot redact it, as we cannot recover the values we override with the redaction marker. Therefore, to avoid leaking sensitive data, broken configs are withheld and not delivered by the API endpoint. This is less convenient for the user, but if they can't edit the config file to fix it, they couldn't have broken it in the first place? #### Tests pmpn check, pnpm test #### Manual Testing Heavy interaction with the config UI, both in "form" and "UI" mode. #### Evidence - Models used: *(N/A - human-authored)* - Submitter effort: *about 16 hours* - Agent notes: PR description drafted by Cog (openclaw/Pony Alpha) from commit message and prior PR context - greptile-requested changes authored by codex and manually reviewed Co-authored-by: Abdel Sy Fane #### Review Hints - restoreRedactedValues() doesn't throw based on outside data. It may thow when another function generates bad objects, but that's a programming error, not a data error. Any exceptions raised for be for coding errors inside the project, not bad data. Those should not be suppressed. Hiding bugs is **BAD**. - When writing the config, it has to be restored using the uihints for the *currently loaded* config, not the newly proposed one. the restore has to match what we handed out to be edited, and the current config doesn't have data to restore values in newly added keys anyway. - regex-based redaction for plugin keys is needed for backwards compatibility. Redaction only acts on string values, see redactObject...() - buildRedactionLookup() adds the path without `[]` *in addition* to the one with. - isAnySchema() seems broken, but not touching this here. New issue has been opened. - keys added by plugins that do not have a uiHint will get one based on their name (schema.ts, applySensitiveHints()). - Wildcard logic when recursing through objects in redact-snapshot.ts is proven working by the tests in redact-snapshot.test.ts. fixes #10090 supersedes #10697

Greptile Overview

Greptile Summary

This PR completes the credential redaction refactor by switching from name/regex-based detection to schema-driven sensitivity. Sensitive fields are now marked in the Zod config schema via `z.registry()` and `.register(sensitive)`, and `mapSensitivePaths()` derives `uiHints.sensitive` from that registry. Gateway config handlers now apply schema-driven redaction consistently: - `config.get` redacts the returned snapshot using the schema’s `uiHints` - write endpoints (`config.set`, `config.patch`, `config.apply`) restore redacted sentinels using the current config + `uiHints`, then validate, write, and redact their responses The UI config form was updated to rely on `uiHints.sensitive` rather than local path heuristics, and env-var placeholders (`${VAR}`) are intentionally not masked/redacted.

Confidence Score: 5/5

- This PR is safe to merge with minimal risk. - Reviewed the redaction/restore logic, schema-driven sensitivity mapping, and config handler wiring. The new schema-derived `uiHints` are consistently applied on read and write endpoints, tests cover wildcard/array/record round-trips, and the UI now defers sensitivity decisions to backend-provided hints. - No files require special attention

items.title: [Feature]: Add Gemini (Google Search grounding) as web_search provider
items.body:
## Summary Adds `"gemini"` as a web_search provider alongside Brave, Perplexity, and Grok, using Gemini's built-in [Google Search grounding](https://ai.google.dev/gemini-api/docs/grounding). Also adds **auto-detection** of search providers when no explicit `provider` is configured. Closes #13074 ## What changed ### `src/agents/tools/web-search.ts` - New `runGeminiSearch()` — calls Gemini `generateContent` with `tools: [{ google_search: {} }]` - `resolveRedirectUrl()` — resolves Google grounding redirect URLs to direct URLs via parallel HEAD requests (5s timeout, graceful fallback) - Gemini config resolution helpers (`resolveGeminiConfig`, `resolveGeminiApiKey`, `resolveGeminiModel`) - Updated `runWebSearch()`, `createWebSearchTool()`, `missingSearchKeyPayload()`, `resolveSearchProvider()` to handle the gemini provider path - **Auto-detection:** When no explicit `provider` is set, checks for available API keys in priority order: Brave → Gemini → Perplexity → Grok. Falls back to Brave if none found. - **Security:** API key stripped from error messages (`key=***`) to prevent leakage in logs — Gemini REST API requires key as query parameter (no Bearer token alternative without ADC) ### Config types & validation - `types.tools.ts` — added `"gemini"` to provider union, added `gemini` config block - `zod-schema.agent-runtime.ts` — added `"gemini"` to zod validation, added gemini schema - `schema.ts` — updated provider description string ### Tests - 2 new config validation tests (gemini with config, gemini with defaults) - 8 new auto-detection tests (per-provider detection, priority order, explicit override, fallback) - All existing + new tests passing (11 total) ### Docs - `docs/tools/web.md` — added Auto-detection section documenting priority order and fallback behavior ## Config ```json5 { tools: { web: { search: { provider: "gemini", // optional — auto-detected from available keys gemini: { apiKey: "...", // optional if GEMINI_API_KEY env var is set model: "gemini-2.5-flash" // optional, this is the default } } } } } ``` ### Auto-detection When no `provider` is explicitly set, OpenClaw checks for available API keys in this order: 1. **Brave** — `BRAVE_API_KEY` env var or `search.apiKey` config 2. **Gemini** — `GEMINI_API_KEY` env var or `search.gemini.apiKey` config 3. **Perplexity** — `PERPLEXITY_API_KEY` / `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` env var or `search.perplexity.apiKey` config 4. **Grok** — `XAI_API_KEY` env var or `search.grok.apiKey` config If no keys are found, falls back to Brave (existing behavior). ## Testing - [x] `pnpm check` passes (lint + format) - [x] Unit tests pass (11/11) - [x] Live tested against Gemini API — grounding returns content + citations with resolved URLs - [x] Self-review completed — found and fixed API key leakage in error messages before finalizing - [x] AI-assisted (Claude Opus 4.6 via OpenClaw) — fully reviewed and understood ## Design decisions - **URL resolution:** Gemini grounding returns citations through `vertexaisearch.cloud.google.com/grounding-api-redirect/...` redirects. We resolve these to direct URLs via parallel HEAD requests (5s timeout, graceful fallback to redirect URL on failure). This adds latency but gives the agent usable URLs. - **API key in query string:** Gemini REST API requires this (no header alternative without ADC). We strip `key=...` from error messages to prevent log leakage. - **Default model:** `gemini-2.5-flash` — fast, cheap, supports grounding. - **Auto-detection priority:** Brave first (most common existing setup), then Gemini, Perplexity, Grok. Explicit `provider` config always overrides auto-detection. ## Related - #5775 (Grok provider — similar pattern) - #11399 (Extensible providers)

Greptile Overview

Greptile Summary

Adds Gemini as a web search provider with Google Search grounding capability, alongside existing Brave, Perplexity, and Grok providers. Implements auto-detection of search providers based on available API keys when no explicit `provider` is configured. **Key changes:** - New `runGeminiSearch()` function calling Gemini's `generateContent` API with Google Search grounding tool - URL resolution helper (`resolveRedirectUrl()`) to convert Google grounding redirect URLs to direct URLs via parallel HEAD requests (5s timeout, graceful fallback) - Auto-detection logic checking API keys in priority order: Brave → Gemini → Perplexity → Grok, with fallback to Brave - Configuration schema updates across types, zod validation, and documentation - Security: API key redaction in error messages to prevent log leakage (Gemini REST API requires key as query param) - Comprehensive test coverage (11 tests total: 3 config validation, 8 auto-detection scenarios) **Implementation follows existing patterns:** - Consistent with Grok/Perplexity provider structure - Proper `externalContent` metadata included - API keys marked as sensitive in zod schema - Config help and labels added for new Gemini fields - Documentation updated with setup instructions and auto-detection behavior All previously reported issues from review threads have been addressed (error message redaction, JSON parse guards, config schema entries).

Confidence Score: 5/5

- This PR is safe to merge with minimal risk - Implementation is thorough and follows established patterns in the codebase. All previously identified issues (API key leakage, JSON parse guards, missing config entries) have been addressed. The code includes proper error handling, security measures (API key redaction), comprehensive test coverage (11/11 passing), and maintains consistency with existing provider implementations. Auto-detection logic is well-tested with 8 test cases covering priority order and fallback behavior. - No files require special attention Last reviewed commit: cfca253

items.title: fix: unify session maintenance and cron run pruning
items.body:
## Summary
- Combine session store maintenance (prune, cap, rotate) with cron run session pruning in one change set.
- Make maintenance warn-only by default, warn the active session, and allow strict enforcement via config.
- Support duration and size thresholds (`pruneAfter`, `rotateBytes`) with new parsing helpers and docs/examples.
- Ensure cron pruning works in multi-agent setups and reuse shared session-key utilities.

## Detailed
### What changed
- **Session maintenance (sessions.json)**
  - Warn-only default behavior; enforcement is opt-in with `session.maintenance.mode: "enforce"`.
  - Warns only when the **active session** would be evicted, and sends that warning to the session’s last known channel (best-effort delivery with a system-event fallback).
  - Maintenance thresholds now accept duration and size strings:
    - `pruneAfter: "30m" | "1h" | "30d"`
    - `rotateBytes: "10kb" | "1mb" | "10mb"`
  - Legacy `pruneDays` remains supported but is deprecated in favor of `pruneAfter`.

- **Cron run pruning**
  - Adds a cron session reaper with per-store throttling.
  - Sweeps per agent store path, so multi-agent cron runs are pruned correctly.
  - Consolidates cron run key detection into a shared helper.

- **Docs and examples**
  - Updated `docs/gateway/configuration.md` and `docs/gateway/configuration-examples.md` with new fields and examples.

### How information is routed
- **Active session** is determined by the resolved `sessionKey` for the inbound message (in `initSessionState`).
- **Maintenance warning routing** uses existing outbound plumbing:
  `saveSessionStoreUnlocked` → `getActiveSessionMaintenanceWarning` → `deliverSessionMaintenanceWarning` →
  `resolveSessionDeliveryTarget` (last channel + target) → `deliverOutboundPayloads` → channel adapter.
  If a target cannot be resolved, the warning is queued via `enqueueSystemEvent` and prefixed on the next prompt.

### Reasoning
- `sessions.json` can grow without bound, causing heavy synchronous parses and slow writes. Pruning and capping keep it bounded, and rotation protects against runaway file sizes.
- Cron run sessions are intentionally ephemeral but accumulate quickly in automation-heavy setups. A dedicated reaper keeps those entries from crowding out real conversations.
- Warn-only default prevents surprise resets, while still surfacing problems early to the user. Enforcement remains a single config switch.

### Behavior and safeguards
- Pruning/capping only runs on store writes and skips when `skipMaintenance` is set (migrations).
- Warn-only is strictly **active-session scoped**, and does not evict anything.
- Cron reaper key matching is anchored to canonical cron run keys.
- Per-store throttling prevents repeated expensive sweeps in multi-agent setups.

## Notes
- supersedes #12920 and #12588
- thanks @skyfallsin and @Glucksberg

items.title: fix(media): strip MEDIA: prefix in loadWebMediaInternal
items.body:
## Summary - Strip `MEDIA:` prefix (with optional whitespace) in `loadWebMediaInternal()` before processing the URL, preventing ENOENT errors when agent tools return `MEDIA:`-prefixed local paths ## Problem Agent tools like TTS return media paths with a `MEDIA:` prefix (e.g., `MEDIA:/tmp/tts-xxx/voice.mp3`). When the agent passes this URL to a channel sender (e.g., Telegram `sendMessage`), `loadWebMediaInternal()` receives the raw prefix and attempts `fs.readFile("MEDIA:/tmp/...")`, causing: ``` ENOENT: no such file or directory, open 'MEDIA:/tmp/tts-xsMcDl/voice-1769961421124.mp3' ``` The function already strips `file://` and resolves `~` prefixes but did not handle the `MEDIA:` convention. ## Changes **`src/web/media.ts`** - Add `MEDIA:` prefix detection and stripping at the top of `loadWebMediaInternal()`, consistent with existing `file://` handling **`src/web/media.test.ts`** - Add test: strips `MEDIA:` prefix before reading local file - Add test: strips `MEDIA:` prefix with leading whitespace after colon ## Test plan - [x] Both new tests fail before the fix, pass after - [x] All 12 existing `media.test.ts` tests continue to pass - [x] All 9 `media/parse.test.ts` tests pass (no regression in text parser) - [x] `pnpm build` passes - [x] `pnpm check` passes (0 warnings, 0 errors) Fixes #6383

Greptile Overview

Greptile Summary

This change updates `loadWebMediaInternal()` to accept agent-produced media references that are prefixed with `MEDIA:` by stripping that prefix (including optional whitespace) before any path/URL handling. This prevents local reads from attempting to open paths like `MEDIA:/tmp/...` and aligns `loadWebMediaInternal()` with other parts of the media pipeline that already treat `MEDIA:` as a tagging convention. The PR also adds two unit tests in `src/web/media.test.ts` to ensure `MEDIA:` and `MEDIA: ` both successfully load a local file as media, covering the regression that produced ENOENT errors when sending tool-produced media via channel senders.

Confidence Score: 5/5

- This PR is safe to merge with minimal risk. - The change is narrowly scoped (prefix normalization only) and is covered by new tests. Stripping `MEDIA:` occurs before existing URL/path handling and does not bypass the HTTP fetch + SSRF policy path; `MEDIA:http(s)://...` still flows through the remote fetch branch. - No files require special attention **Context used:** - Context from `dashboard` - CLAUDE.md ([source](https://app.greptile.com/review/custom-context?memory=fd949e91-5c3a-4ab5-90a1-cbe184fd6ce8)) - Context from `dashboard` - AGENTS.md ([source](https://app.greptile.com/review/custom-context?memory=0d0c8278-ef8e-4d6c-ab21-f5527e322f13))
